preFix.title: Willow hanging basket liner
preFix.subtitle: A woven willow liner for a 350 mm wire hanging basket
preFix.excerpt: A willow liner woven directly onto a 350 mm wire hanging basket frame. The willow rods replace the usual coir or moss liner and last for several seasons outdoors. The project uses the frame as the mould and introduces weaving onto a pre-made structure.
preFix.sourceNotes: Cooke The Basketmaker (1882) for the randing technique used against a wire frame; Beeton's Book of Household Management (1861) for the kitchen garden hanging basket.

content.text: A woven willow liner fits inside a standard wire hanging basket and lasts longer than compressed coir or moss. The wire frame acts as the mould:
rods are threaded between the wire uprights of the frame and woven around them in a simple
weave. No separate staking is needed; the wire is the structure. This is a good first willow project for beginners who do not yet have a full willow toolkit.

A 350 mm wire hanging basket frame with at least ten wire uprights; approximately 200 g of fine brown buff willow rods at 2 to 3 mm diameter; secateurs; soaking trough; bodkin. Soak all rods for forty-five minutes and mellow for twenty minutes before use.
Set the basket frame upside down on a flat surface. Begin weaving at the base: take a long soaked

and start it behind one wire upright at the bottom ring. Weave in front of one wire, behind the next, all the way around the base ring. Lock the start by weaving past it by three uprights, then trim the tail flush. The first two rows at the base ring need to be pulled tight to hold their position.

Continue randing upward, working toward the rim. Each row should sit snugly against the one below. Add new weavers at an upright: start the new rod behind the same upright where the old rod ended, overlapping for four uprights. The
ensures the rods bend around the curved wire frame without cracking. Weave to within 30 mm of the rim.

At the top, cut each weaver end to a point with secateurs and tuck the tip through the weaving below the rim wire using the bodkin to secure it. The liner does not need a separate border; the rim wire of the basket holds the top edge. Trim any protruding tips flush.

Line the inside of the woven liner with hessian or a perforated plastic disc before adding compost, to prevent soil washing through. At the end of the season, remove the plants and compost, brush the liner clean, and store dry. A well-made willow liner lasts two to four seasons before the rods become brittle.

initialFindings.message: PATTERN tutorial has no Method/Steps section and no orderedList — sequential instructions missing
